4  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

5 Model building and a mini case study

The earlier chapters developed the pieces of logistic regression one at a time. This chapter brings them together into a short analysis workflow:

  1. Define the event.
  2. Choose a small set of interpretable predictors.
  3. Fit and compare models.
  4. Report effects as odds ratios and predicted probabilities.
  5. Check predictive performance.
  6. Communicate limitations.

The goal is not to find the “best possible” student-performance model. The goal is to practise a defensible modelling workflow.

5.1 Define the outcome

We define the event as passing the final mathematics grade:

\[ Y_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } G3_i \ge 10,\\ 0 & \text{if } G3_i < 10. \end{cases} \]

  • Output
  • Code

  0   1 
130 265 

        0         1 
0.3291139 0.6708861 
table(students$pass_exam)
prop.table(table(students$pass_exam))

Always report the event definition and the event rate. Different cutoffs can answer different questions.

5.2 Choose predictors

A compact first model uses:

  • studytime: weekly study-time category.
  • failures: number of previous class failures.
  • absences: number of school absences.

These predictors are easy to explain and available before the final grade is known.

  • Output
  • Code

Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences, family = binomial, 
    data = students)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)  1.08795    0.32965   3.300 0.000966 ***
studytime    0.03980    0.13931   0.286 0.775104    
failures    -0.96217    0.16995  -5.662  1.5e-08 ***
absences    -0.01843    0.01332  -1.384 0.166398    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 455.11  on 391  degrees of freedom
AIC: 463.11

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
fit_case <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences,
                data = students,
                family = binomial)
summary(fit_case)

Odds ratios give a first interpretation:

  • Output
  • Code
             estimate     2.5 %    97.5 %
(Intercept) 2.9681706 1.5555872 5.6634800
studytime   1.0406030 0.7919686 1.3672947
failures    0.3820611 0.2738250 0.5330802
absences    0.9817405 0.9564488 1.0077010
exp(cbind(
  estimate = coef(fit_case),
  confint.default(fit_case)
))
Key-point

Use odds ratios to summarise model coefficients, but use predicted probabilities to communicate the practical size of effects.

5.3 Compare a small set of models

We compare three models:

  • Output
  • Code
          df      AIC
fit_null   1 502.5040
fit_study  2 502.2730
fit_case   4 463.1066
Analysis of Deviance Table

Model 1: pass_exam ~ studytime
Model 2: pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences
  Resid. Df Resid. Dev Df Deviance  Pr(>Chi)    
1       393     498.27                          
2       391     455.11  2   43.166 4.232e-10 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
fit_null <- glm(pass_exam ~ 1, data = students, family = binomial)
fit_study <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime, data = students, family = binomial)
fit_case <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences,
                data = students,
                family = binomial)

AIC(fit_null, fit_study, fit_case)
anova(fit_study, fit_case, test = "Chisq")

This mirrors the model-building logic from the Linear Regression Short Course: start simple, add predictors for clear reasons, and check whether the added complexity is useful.

5.4 Consider interactions carefully

An interaction allows the effect of one predictor to depend on another. For example, the association between absences and passing might differ depending on whether a student has previous failures.

  • Output
  • Code
                df      AIC
fit_case         4 463.1066
fit_interaction  5 463.9819

Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime + failures * high_absence, 
    family = binomial, data = students)

Coefficients:
                         Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)               0.79818    0.34899   2.287   0.0222 * 
studytime                 0.04476    0.13851   0.323   0.7466   
failures                 -0.67971    0.23208  -2.929   0.0034 **
high_absencelow           0.27664    0.25978   1.065   0.2869   
failures:high_absencelow -0.59156    0.35360  -1.673   0.0943 . 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 453.98  on 390  degrees of freedom
AIC: 463.98

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
fit_interaction <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures * high_absence,
                       data = students,
                       family = binomial)

AIC(fit_case, fit_interaction)
summary(fit_interaction)

Interactions can be useful, but they make interpretation harder and require enough data in each subgroup.

Note

Add interaction terms when they answer a real question or are strongly motivated by the context. Do not add them only to search for significance.

5.5 Communicate with scenarios

Scenario predictions translate the model back to the probability scale.

  • Output
  • Code
  studytime failures absences predicted_probability
1         1        0        2             0.7485492
2         2        0        2             0.7559662
3         4        0        2             0.7703504
scenarios <- data.frame(
  studytime = c(1, 2, 4),
  failures = c(0, 0, 0),
  absences = c(2, 2, 2)
)

scenarios$predicted_probability <- predict(
  fit_case,
  newdata = scenarios,
  type = "response"
)

scenarios

For a non-technical audience, this table is usually more useful than a coefficient table.

5.6 Summarise predictive performance

  • Output
  • Code
         Actual
Predicted   0   1
        0  36  18
        1  94 247
   accuracy sensitivity specificity 
  0.7164557   0.9320755   0.2769231 
prob <- predict(fit_case, type = "response")
threshold <- 0.5
pred <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
cm <- table(
  Predicted = factor(pred, levels = c(0, 1)),
  Actual = factor(students$pass_exam, levels = c(0, 1))
)
cm

accuracy <- sum(diag(cm)) / sum(cm)
sensitivity <- cm["1", "1"] / sum(cm[, "1"])
specificity <- cm["0", "0"] / sum(cm[, "0"])

c(accuracy = accuracy,
  sensitivity = sensitivity,
  specificity = specificity)

This is an in-sample summary, so it is optimistic. In a real predictive project, we would use a validation set or cross-validation.

5.7 Reporting template

Use this structure for a short logistic regression report:

  1. Outcome: Define the event and report the event rate.
  2. Model: State the predictors and the link function.
  3. Effects: Report key odds ratios with confidence intervals.
  4. Predictions: Give predicted probabilities for realistic scenarios.
  5. Performance: Report threshold, confusion matrix metrics, and any AUC or validation results.
  6. Limitations: Note missing predictors, observational design, sample size, class imbalance, and any modelling assumptions.

Template paragraph:

We modelled the probability of passing the final mathematics assessment using logistic regression. The event was defined as G3 >= 10. The model included study time, previous failures, and absences. Coefficients were estimated on the log-odds scale and reported as odds ratios. To make the results easier to interpret, we also calculated predicted probabilities for representative student profiles. Using a threshold of 0.5, we summarised classification performance with accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. These performance results are in-sample and should be validated before using the model for decision making.

5.8 Chapter summary

Logistic regression analysis follows the same disciplined pattern as linear regression: define the response, build interpretable models, compare alternatives, check assumptions and performance, and communicate effects on a scale the audience can understand. The main difference is the scale: coefficients live on the log-odds scale, while conclusions are usually clearest as predicted probabilities.
